Sets a timestamp to the end of a specified time unit, effectively setting all smaller components to their maximum values. For example, EndOf with unit 'day' sets hours to 23, minutes to 59, seconds to 59, and milliseconds to 999.

## Parameters

- **isoTimestamp** (String, required): The ISO timestamp to adjust
  - Type: String (ISO 8601 format)
  - Required: Yes

- **unit** (String, required): The time unit to set to end of
  - Type: String
  - Required: Yes
  - Options: 'year', 'month', 'week', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', 'second'

## Returns

**Type**: String

**Output Event**: output
- **result** (String): An ISO timestamp set to the end of the specified unit

## Behavior

- EndOf('day') sets time to 23:59:59.999 while preserving the date
- EndOf('hour') sets minutes to 59, seconds to 59, milliseconds to 999
- EndOf('month') sets day to the last day of the month and time to 23:59:59.999
- EndOf('year') sets month to December, day to 31, and time to 23:59:59.999
- The original timestamp is not modified; a new timestamp is returned

## Use Cases

- Creating inclusive end boundaries for date range queries
- Setting period-end timestamps for reporting intervals
- Implementing ceiling-style rounding for time-based bucketing
- Determining the last possible moment of a day for deadline comparisons
- Creating end-of-period markers for data partitioning and archiving

System.Date.TimeAsObject.description: Converts timestamp to an object with components
System.Date.TimeAsObject.documentation:
# System.Date.TimeAsObject

Converts a timestamp into an object with individual named properties for each date and time component. The resulting object contains fields such as year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and millisecond, making it easy to access individual components.

## Parameters

- **isoTimestamp** (String, required): The ISO timestamp to decompose into components
  - Type: String (ISO 8601 format)
  - Required: Yes

## Returns

**Type**: Object

**Output Event**: output
- **result** (Object): An object with named properties for each date/time component (e.g., year, month, day, hour, minute, second, millisecond)

## Behavior

- Decomposes the timestamp into individual named fields
- Each component is accessible as a separate property on the result object
- The property names follow a consistent naming convention
- All components are numeric values

## Use Cases

- Accessing individual date components by name for display or calculation
- Building custom date display formats by selecting specific components
- Performing conditional logic based on multiple date components simultaneously
- Passing date components as separate parameters to other functions
- Creating structured date data for form population or data binding

System.Date.TimeAsArray.description: Converts timestamp to an array of components
System.Date.TimeAsArray.documentation:
# System.Date.TimeAsArray

Converts a timestamp into an array of individual date and time component values. The resulting array contains the numeric values for year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and millisecond in a fixed positional order.

## Parameters

- **isoTimestamp** (String, required): The ISO timestamp to decompose into an array of components
  - Type: String (ISO 8601 format)
  - Required: Yes

## Returns

**Type**: Array of Number

**Output Event**: output
- **result** (Array): An array of numeric values representing [year, month, day, hour, minute, second, millisecond] in positional order

## Behavior

- Decomposes the timestamp into individual component values in a fixed-position array
- The order of elements is consistent across invocations (typically year, month, day, hour, minute, second, millisecond)
- All elements are numeric values
- This is a more compact representation than TimeAsObject

## Use Cases

- Efficiently accessing date components by index for calculations
- Passing date components as a compact array to other functions
- Comparing individual date components across multiple timestamps
- Building custom date formats by selecting array positions
- Converting timestamps into array format for data processing pipelines

System.JSON.JSONParse.description: Parses a JSON string into an object
System.JSON.JSONParse.documentation:
# System.JSON.JSONParse

Parses a JSON-formatted string and converts it into the corresponding JavaScript/runtime object, array, or primitive value. This is the primary function for deserializing JSON text into usable data structures.

## Parameters

- **jsonString** (String, required): The JSON string to parse
  - Type: String
  - Required: Yes
  - Must be valid JSON syntax
  - Can represent any JSON type: objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, or null
  - Invalid JSON will cause a parse error

## Returns

**Type**: Any (depends on the JSON content)

**Output Event**: output
- **result** (Any): The parsed value, which could be an Object, Array, String, Number, Boolean, or null depending on the JSON content

## Behavior

- Parses the string according to the JSON specification
- Objects become runtime objects, arrays become runtime arrays, etc.
- Throws an error if the input is not valid JSON
- Empty strings or non-JSON text will cause a parse error
- Preserves the full structure including nested objects and arrays

## Use Cases

- Parsing API response bodies that are returned as JSON strings
- Deserializing configuration data stored as JSON text
- Converting JSON data from file reads or database queries into objects
- Processing webhook payloads or message queue payloads that arrive as JSON strings
- Extracting structured data from JSON-encoded string fields

System.JSON.JSONStringify.description: Converts an object to a JSON string
System.JSON.JSONStringify.documentation:
# System.JSON.JSONStringify

Converts a runtime value (object, array, or primitive) into its JSON string representation. This is the primary function for serializing data structures into JSON text format. This is the inverse of JSONParse.

## Parameters

- **source** (Any, required): The value to convert to a JSON string
  - Type: Any (Object, Array, String, Number, Boolean, null)
  - Required: Yes
  - Can be any JSON-serializable value
  - Circular references may cause errors
  - Undefined values and functions are typically excluded from the output

## Returns

**Type**: String

**Output Event**: output
- **result** (String): The JSON string representation of the input value

## Behavior

- Serializes the value into a valid JSON string
- Objects become JSON objects with quoted keys, arrays become JSON arrays
- Strings are escaped and quoted, numbers and booleans are rendered as literals
- Null values become the JSON literal null
- Nested structures are recursively serialized
- The result is always a valid JSON string that can be parsed back with JSONParse

## Use Cases

- Serializing request bodies for API calls that expect JSON payloads
- Converting data objects to JSON strings for storage in databases or files
- Creating JSON-formatted configuration strings for external systems
- Logging data structures as readable JSON text for debugging
- Preparing data for transmission through messaging systems that use JSON format
